Dear Miss Foster:
Thank you for your letter of June 22 concerning political
body sponsorship of a fund raising dinner in connection with
a statewide initiative petition.
There is no restriction on whether a political body can
sponsor fund raising events for political purposes, and no
requirmnent that a political body file papers IEquesting
approval of the event with a public agency.
Therefore, there
appears to be no question of the legality of holding the
event referred to in your letteri
There is, however, a question of whether the sponsors of the
Evant have incurrad reporting duties under the Political Reform
Act of 1914.
The money appears to have been raised in support
or opposition to a statewide initiative measure.
Government
Code 82013(a)
defines
3 "Recipient committee" that incurs
reporting duties as one whose "contributions received total
five hundred dollars or more in a Calendar yearn"
The issue at this point involves questions of whether a
reporting duty has been incurred, and if so, which body has
the duty of reporting
(is it the "American Party" or the
"Committee for Special Event"),
and what filing deadlines
apply.
-‘.


.
.

Assistance Division of the Fair political Practices
commission, which will be able to assist you with potential
reporting requirements
Very truly yours,
WILLIAM Ni DURLEY
Assistant to the secretary of state
Elections and Political Reform
RICHARD a. mess

Elections and political Reform

Dear Mr. Maneasl
This is to coniirm my telephone call this date for an appointment
regarding a Clarification on the matter of a political body and/or
party sponsoring a fund raising event in support of an initiative
measure.
Enclosed tor the record is a copy of the flyer issued for this
Purpose.
On may 8, 1976 I called Mr. Arnold's Office on this and was referred
to the Elections Division and subsequently to someone in your office
The information given me at that time on the phone was in effect;
"There is nothing in the Elections Rerorm Act making it illegal for
a political party to sponsor a fund raising event in support of an
Initiative Measure. However.
it is required that the Committee in
charge of the event must file a report with the Secretary of State as
to the amount a! the funds raised when the amount is over $ 500.50".
Your cooperation in providing this information in writing is requested
as the authority needed for the Committee's compliance as a record
for our files and will be greatly appreciated.
Thank you.
sincerely yours,

sections.question: You have asked whether you, as treasurer for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.
sections.conclusion: If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an accounting of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not register as a committee under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent" and the party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the Committee for Special Events is not "agent" of the Party, they may qualify as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a)) with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office and file campaign statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the American Party, as treasurer, must register as a committee under the Political Reform Act when sponsoring a fundraising dinner for Proposition 13 where a subcommittee (Committee for Special Events) receives and manages all funds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Registration obligation depends on whether the American Party receives accounting from the Committee for Special Events. If no accounting is provided, the Party need not register. If accounting is provided, the Party assumes reporting obligations as the Committee becomes its agent; alternatively, the Committee for Special Events may independently qualify as a recipient committee with separate filing obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple duplicate pages and handwritten dates. Cover letter from Secretary of State Elections Division (June 29, 1978) and initial inquiry letter (June 22, 1978) are included before the main FPPC advice letter dated July 18, 1978. Minor OCR errors present but main Q/A sections clearly identifiable. Date discrepancy: letterhead shows 'July 18, 1919' but context indicates 1978.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether you, as treasurer for the American Party, have an obligation to file as a committee under the Political Reform Act due to your sponsorship of this event.

CONCLUSION: If you, as treasurer of the American Party, do not receive an accounting of the funds received or spent by the Committee for Special Events chaired by Wilma Shirley the Party need not register as a committee under the law simply because it sponsored the event. If the Committee for Special Events, however, chooses to provide you with an accounting and other substantiating records pertaining to the event, the Committee for Special Events may then assume the role of the Party's "agent" and the party will therefore assume the reporting obligation. If the Committee for Special Events is not "agent" of the Party, they may qualify as a recipient committee (under Government Code Section 82013(a)) with an obligation to register with the Secretary of State's Office and file campaign statements.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether the American Party must register as a committee for sponsoring a Proposition 13 fundraising dinner managed by its subcommittee (Committee for Special Events). The registration requirement depends on whether the Party receives accounting records from the subcommittee, determining whether an agent relationship exists.
